title: Pope Leo XIV's Encyclical on AI and Human Dignity (Magnifica Humanitas)
notes: NYT coverage of Pope Leo XIV's first encyclical on safeguarding the human person in the age of artificial intelligence, including calls for AI regulation and worker protections.

title: AI Loss of Control Incidents and Extinction Risk
notes: AI Governance and Safety Canada's Executive Director Wyatt Tessari L'Allié tells Canadian MPs that AI loss of control incidents are already emerging, that experts warn AI poses a risk of human extinction, and that Canada should spearhead talks to prevent the threat.

title: CRAFT Resources
notes: A growing collection of AI education resources and lessons across AI and society arts science language arts math and history-social science.

title: Critical Big Data and Algorithmic Literacy Network
authors: Critical Big Data and Algorithmic Literacy Network
notes: An international network and resource collection focused on critical thinking, ethical reflection, and education for datafication, big data, algorithms, and AI.

title: Teaching about Data: A Resource for Educators
notes: An educator resource for teaching critical data literacy, including how data and technology shape power, autonomy, public debate, and social decision-making.

title: Ethics & Algorithms Toolkit
authors: GovEx, City and County of San Francisco, Harvard DataSmart, and Data Community DC
notes: A practitioner-facing risk management toolkit for governments and other organizations to assess algorithmic risk, discuss impacts with stakeholders, and plan mitigation strategies.

title: Data Ethics Decision Aid (DEDA)
notes: An open-access toolkit that helps data analysts, project managers, and policymakers identify ethical and practical issues in data and AI projects, document deliberation, and support accountability.

title: Automating Inequality: How High-Tech Tools Profile Police and Punish the Poor
notes: Detailed case studies of predictive risk systems in public services — useful for tracing how threshold choices translate into scrutiny, investigation, and intervention for specific populations.

title: Machine Bias
authors: Julia Angwin, Jeff Larson, Surya Mattu, Lauren Kirchner
notes: The foundational investigation of COMPAS risk scores in criminal sentencing — shows how threshold choices interact with racial disparities in false positive and false negative rates.

title: Fairness and Machine Learning
authors: Solon Barocas, Moritz Hardt, Arvind Narayanan
notes: Chapter 2 is especially useful for understanding how different fairness definitions involve different threshold tradeoffs — and why it is mathematically impossible to satisfy all definitions simultaneously.

title: The Perpetual Line-Up
authors: Clare Garvie, Alvaro Bedoya, Jonathan Frankle
notes: Systematic analysis of police use of face recognition, including the threshold and accuracy problems that lead to wrongful identifications.

title: Sorting Things Out: Classification and Its Consequences
authors: Geoffrey C. Bowker and Susan Leigh Star
notes: The foundational STS text on how classification systems become infrastructure. Especially useful on how categories naturalize over time and how the experience of one group becomes the invisible default.

title: Critical Questions for Big Data
notes: Covers scale, context, bias, and the mistaken belief that large datasets are automatically more objective. Also useful for context collapse and how data is contextually produced rather than found.

title: Algorithms of Oppression: How Search Engines Reinforce Racism
notes: Documents how algorithmic systems encode and reproduce racial bias through their construction and the datasets they rely on.

title: Gender Shades: Intersectional Accuracy Disparities in Commercial Gender Classification
notes: The foundational empirical study of accuracy disparities in commercial face analysis systems across gender and skin tone.

title: Invisible Women: Data Bias in a World Designed for Men
notes: An accessible survey of how the male default in data collection and system design produces systematic harm — useful for illustrating the pattern across many domains at once.

title: Privacy in Context: Technology Policy and the Integrity of Social Life
notes: Introduces contextual integrity: the argument that privacy violations occur when information flows violate the norms of the context in which data was originally shared.

title: The Age of Surveillance Capitalism
notes: Argues that the core logic of major platforms is the systematic appropriation and repurposing of behavioral data as a commodity, regardless of user expectations or awareness.

title: Raw Data Is an Oxymoron (Introduction)
notes: A clear starting point for the claim that data are always made through interpretation, classification, and context.

title: The Hidden Biases in Big Data
notes: A short accessible piece using Street Bump to show how data collection reflects unequal participation.

title: To Predict and Serve?
notes: A clear case study of predictive policing and how police data reproduces past enforcement patterns.

title: The Parable of Google Flu: Traps in Big Data Analysis
authors: David Lazer, Ryan Kennedy, Gary King, Alessandro Vespignani
notes: Discusses platform-generated data, proxy measurement, and the limits of large-scale behavioral traces.

title: Data Feminism
authors: Catherine D'Ignazio and Lauren F. Klein
notes: Especially useful for thinking about missing data, power, standpoint, and the politics of counting.

title: Making Up People
notes: On how human classification categories feed back into the behavior of the people classified — what Hacking calls the looping effect.

title: Race After Technology
notes: Extends the classification-as-production argument into algorithmic systems, coining the New Jim Code to describe how technical categories can reproduce social hierarchies.

title: The Color of Law
notes: A historical account of how zoning and land use classification produced residential segregation — useful for connecting algorithmic classification to longer histories of boundary-drawing.

title: Code/Space
notes: On how software classification systems produce space and geometry rather than simply representing them.

title: Trump Administration Investigating Gender Treatments at Mount Sinai
notes: Grand jury subpoena demanded patient records from Mount Sinai for adolescents who received gender-related care — medical data collected for treatment was sought by a federal investigation. Second Manhattan hospital after NYU Langone to receive such a subpoena.

title: Relying on AI at work reduces self-efficacy, ownership, and meaning while active collaboration mitigates the effects
authors: Elena Hayoung Lee, Yidan Yin, Nan Jia, and Cheryl J. Wakslak
notes: Pre-registered experiment (N=269) and follow-up survey (N=270) showing passive AI use (copying AI output) undermines self-efficacy, psychological ownership, and work meaningfulness — effects persist after returning to manual work. Active collaboration (draft first, refine with AI) preserved psychological connection comparable to independent work. Scientific Reports vol. 16, article 13583.

title: The AI Resist List
notes: A curated list of actions, campaigns, and alternative visions that resist large-scale AI development by organizing around narrative, funding, data, data centers, resource extraction, labor, adoption, surveillance, and policy.

title: Images of AI library
notes: A Creative Commons image library offering alternatives to stock AI imagery, with attribution guidance and searchable images for teaching how visual metaphors shape public understanding of AI.

title: Report algorithmic discrimination!
notes: A reporting and advocacy resource that explains algorithmic discrimination, invites affected people to document incidents, and shows how firsthand reports can support investigations and stronger safeguards.
